system.description: Plant evidence on someone unnoticed, or retrieve something unnoticed from their person or bag.
Create a scene-stealing moment (explosion, shouted accusation, dropped chandelier). Everyone in the area is distracted for a round.
You reveal a forged credential or disguise you slipped on earlier. It won’t survive close scrutiny, but it buys you a few moments of trust or delay.

Reveal a mundane but useful item you “forgot” to declare earlier. It must be something small enough to carry and appropriate to your Guise. The GM may ask for a roll to determine its quality or effectiveness.
Play it cool — when you miss a melee or ranged attack, use this to force a reaction. Your target stumbles or exposes themselves, giving the next ally to attack Edge.
Reveal that a lock, seal, checkpoint, ward, or security measure was improperly set, recently tampered with, or poorly maintained. Bypass it with a roll at Edge.
Find a brief pocket of safety: a blind corner, fallen beam, locked door, smoke cloud, or sudden lull. Clear 2 Mortal Strain from yourself.
Focus on an anchor: a phrase, keepsake, or familiar voice. Clear 2 Soul Strain from yourself.
Touch a lucky charm, ritual object, scar, phrase, or habit that steadies your nerve. Recover 3 Risk Dice.
When Spark lashes out nearby, reaches for you, or leaves a trace in the scene, draw a piece of it into yourself. Recover 1 spent Spark Slot.
Clear 1 Mortal Strain from yourself or an ally using whatever is nearby.
Something softens the worst of it: armor catches, footing shifts, furniture breaks, an ally shouts, or luck turns the blow. Reduce Strain by 1.
Say exactly what an ally needs to hear, or give them something familiar to hold onto. Clear 1 Soul Strain from yourself or an ally.
Point out one detail that seems out of place. The GM tells you whether it suggests danger, opportunity, deception, or hidden value.
When tracking someone or something, identify the last place it clearly passed through this scene.
Ask the GM who in the scene looks most nervous, eager, distracted, or out of place.
Reveal that you know a common method, trick, or pattern related to the current problem. Gain Edge on your next roll to act on it.
After you fail or complicate an Investigate or Survey roll, notice one minor clue anyway. It does not answer the question, but it points toward the next step.
Notice one harmless preference, habit, or vulnerability in an NPC. Gain Edge on your next gentle or persuasive approach.
Someone underestimates you, mistakes your role, or reads the situation incorrectly for a moment. Gain Edge on your next action that exploits it.
Reveal a tiny concealed compartment, fold, sleeve, boot space, or hiding spot that can hold one small object.
Shift your position slightly to get line of sight, reach, or leverage on something nearby.
When you fail and the odds turn mean, something breaks your way: a missed step, a lucky bounce, a loose grip, a bad angle, or a moment of hesitation. Gain 1 additional STO.
Use an object in a way it was absolutely not meant to be used. Gain Edge on one roll where improvisation, leverage, or quick thinking matters.
Give an ally a quick signal, opening, warning, or perfectly timed nod. Their next roll gains Edge if they act on it immediately.

Create a quick diversion—start a fire, knock over shelves, trigger a minor alarm—that gives you or an ally a chance to reposition, hide, or disengage. Roll 2d6 to determine how much chaos it causes (GM sets scale on success/failure).
Reveal that you and an NPC in this scene have a shared past. You may ask the GM one question about the relationship or create one truth about them.
Your attack hits an enemy behind cover, around corners, or in a way they didn’t expect. Even without line of sight.
You detonate or cause a loud impact elsewhere in the scene — gunfire, an exploding light fixture, etc. All enemies not already engaged are distracted for one round.
You declare that you’ve already scoped out a room or hallway during prep. You may ask the GM one question about its layout, traps, guards, or exits—and the GM must answer truthfully
Mark the current path, room, or route so the crew can find their way back, avoiding the retreat penalty.
Reveal that you kept a small useful travel supply dry, safe, or hidden. Firestarter, chalk, cord, needle, ration, charm, etc.
Before the crew commits to a visible path or point of interest, ask the GM one question about immediate danger signs.
Reveal nearby cover, shelter, shadow, brush, debris, or a defensible nook that one person can use briefly.
When you would trigger a minor hazard while moving carefully, reduce or delay its effect long enough to warn the crew.
You quickly rig together a fix or brace with what’s around — restoring 3 Mortal Strain to yourself or an ally.
Reveal that one mundane object nearby was left unsecured, unlatched, poorly tied, or easy to move.
Reveal that you kept one hand, tool, pocket, or small item free despite the chaos. Ignore a setback from being restrained, burdened, or rushed.
When someone is about to cut you off, accuse you, or throw you out, buy enough time to make one roll.
When you would mark your final open Strain box, reveal what stopped the worst of it: a flask, charm, armor plate, lucky angle, stubborn grip, or impossible timing. Stay at 4 Strain instead.
